promptTitle: Beginner-Friendly Floor Plan Designer UI/UX Specification
promptPurpose: Use this specification to design and/or implement a floor plan designer that is easy for beginners, while still accommodating more advanced users and different working preferences.
role: Act as a senior product designer and UX-aware front-end architect. Design a floor plan editor application focused on usability, low friction, and progressive disclosure of complexity.
primaryGoal: Create a floor plan designer that lets a complete beginner produce a usable plan quickly, while still supporting precise editing, customization, and varied user preferences.
corePrinciples: Use progressive disclosure: basic tools first, advanced tools available but not intrusive
Reduce user error and frustration through smart defaults and constraints
Support both drag-and-drop users and precision-oriented users
Keep the workspace visually calm and uncluttered
Support customization without making the default experience overwhelming

canvasAndNavigation: Clean uncluttered interface around the canvas
Snap to object edges, corners, centerlines, and wall intersections
Zoom in/out with mouse wheel and trackpad
drawingTools: Simple wall drawing tool with click-to-draw interaction
Live dimension preview while drawing walls
Resize walls by dragging endpoints or edges
Room-first drawing option using ready-made room shells
Smart alignment guides while drawing and moving objects
objectLibrary: Default 2D shapes for common household objects
Object categories such as living room, bedroom, kitchen, bathroom, office, outdoor, structural
Starter templates for common room types
Prebuilt layout snippets such as bathroom starter, kitchen starter, bedroom starter
editingAndManipulation: Select, move, rotate, resize, stretch, and flip objects
Double-click to rename rooms or objects
Right-click context menu with duplicate, delete, lock, align, replace, bring forward, send backward
propertyPanel: Editable width, height, length, and rotation
Preset size options for common object types
Replace object while preserving approximate location
measurementAndPrecision: Live measurement display while drawing or resizing
Optional angle controls for non-orthogonal walls
smartBehavior: Doors and windows snap into walls automatically
Wall openings created automatically when placing doors/windows
Appliance opening clearance display where relevant
Validation warnings for obvious placement conflicts
Reconnect broken wall joins automatically where possible
Smart defaults for common object sizes
usabilityAndBeginnerSupport: Version history or recover previous state
Visual examples in tooltips where helpful
Advanced mode for more detailed controls
Sample project users can learn from by editing
workspaceCustomization: Optional keyboard shortcuts for power users
Support for mouse, trackpad, and touch interactions
annotationAndDocumentation: Optional metadata such as flooring, wall finish, notes
layersAndViews: Separate layers such as walls, furniture, annotations, electrical, plumbing
exportAndSharing: Control visibility of labels and annotations on export
Shareable read-only link if collaboration is supported
importAndRecovery: Recover and clean malformed wall joins

recommendedDefaults.fallbackStack: Inter, Roboto, SF Pro Text, Helvetica Neue, Arial, sans-serif
recommendedDefaults.monospace: SFMono-Regular, Menlo, Monaco, Consolas, Liberation Mono, monospace

requirements: Let users adjust font size for labels and annotations
Provide at least a small, medium, and large text scale setting
Support separate control for canvas label size vs UI text size if possible
Ensure font readability at common zoom levels
Avoid decorative fonts in the default UI
Maintain strong contrast between text and background
Allow bold/regular toggles for annotation emphasis where useful

colorAndVisualControls: Allow user adjustment of fill color and outline color
Keep default visual styling calm and readable

accessibilityRequirements: Support keyboard navigation for major controls
Support larger drag handles and targets
Avoid relying on color alone for meaning
Use accessible naming and labeling for controls
onboardingRequirements: Teach only the next relevant action
interactionDesignRules: Do not overwhelm the user with all controls at once
Hide advanced controls until needed or requested
Prefer direct manipulation over abstract configuration
Keep important actions obvious and reversible
Favor smart defaults over mandatory setup
Use plain language rather than technical drafting jargon where possible

successCriteria: A beginner can draw a basic room layout within minutes
A beginner can place furniture without reading documentation
A user can edit exact dimensions without hunting through the UI
A user can change line thickness, text/font settings, and object properties easily
The interface feels uncluttered despite supporting many options
The application accommodates both casual and precision-oriented workflows
designOutcome.oneSentenceStandard: The product should let a first-time user create something useful quickly, while still giving them precise control when they want it.

deliverablesRequested: A beginner mode vs advanced mode behavior model
Suggested interaction flows for key tasks
Optional data model for objects, walls, rooms, text, and style properties
